speaker: interviewer
text: The date is Tuesday, July the 30th, 1996. The survivor is Clare Parker. Her last name at birth was Hochhauser. The interviewer is Sharon Tyler. City and state is London. We're in England, and the language of the interview is English. Today's date is Tuesday, July the 30th, 1996. I'm Sharon Tyler. I'm interviewing Clare Parker. We're in London, England. Please tell me your name and spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Thank. My married name is Clare Parker, which is P-A-R-K-E-R. And my maiden name was Klara Hochhauser, which is H-O-C double H-A-U-S-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Klara? How was that spelled.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Klara in Hungary. It's K-L-A-R-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you known by any other names, as well? I mean nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, OK. Please tell me your date of birth and age now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this 1st of January-- is 13th of January, 1932. So I'm 64 now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the city and country of your birth.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Budapest, Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell me what are your earliest memories.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I only remember when things start change from, [SOBBING] from normal [CRYING] to different way of life. Like my father was taken to-- on and off to forced labor, which one was before I was 11. And where we lived is outskirt of Budapest, is Pestszenterzsebet. And that town is a suburb of Budapest. We lived in a house, which was several flats in a courtyard. And, and business my father had there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of business was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was there was polishing metals, like skates and cutleries and things like that, came raw material, [INAUDIBLE] shining. I think was chrome and nickel. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] eventually had to close it down, because he was going away more longer period of time. He had to wear a yellow armband. And I would still go to school [SOBBING] in the school where I was the, the only Jewish child. And I, [SOBBING] I had to go out from the classroom when was-- everybody was Catholic. You know Hungary a Catholic country. A priest came with a long, black frock and told me I have to go out to the corridor for an hour until they learn religion. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] eventually I have been thrown stone at me.

speaker: interviewer
text: By the other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- yeah, children. They laughed at me when I was in school. And was a private teacher also in a suburb, quite a long way, where I went to study at the age of 11, with a teacher who also lost a job because she was Jewish. It was quite a long journey to go, especially in cold weather, because I had to walk quite a distance in the snow and everything to get the bus. And after the end of the bus journey, I had to go quite a distance the same way back. And eventually, that had to stop. And the business had to close, because my mother didn't know what to do with it. And I remember in Hungary they were setting up a special place for dealing with us, which was called [INAUDIBLE], which is a place, but in a different meaning of place. They specialized for dealing with Jews. And they collected up everything worth like car, my father's car, and bicycle, fur coats, which in Hungary very cold. The people was wearing before the war. It was very natural thing to wear. And even I had fur coat as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when this happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was, I think, '43, sometime. And then my father's coat, which was fur-lined, and demanded on jewelry. And my mother given everything what we had, but they didn't believe it. And I remember cutting open pillows and feathers everywhere, because they thought it might have been hidden jewelry there. And then it came the time I had to wear yellow star on our clothes, which meant for a few hours a day being able to go out. But again, I don't know if it was everywhere. But in, in this particular place, the corner shops which we bought food, it has been in a pre-printed forms which is on the door were stuck on-- dogs and Jews not allowed in. So I don't know where from we had the food really, must have somewhere. And-- From there, we was collected up and had to go to an area, which is called ghetto. Everybody was all crammed up. So furniture and everything went-- we had to leave behind.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we get to that point, is there-- tell me a bit about your, your family. I mean, there was your parents and just yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, no-- no brothers and sisters. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: --but I had my mother's side grandmother lived in the same town. My father's brother lived in the same town, and my mother's brother. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my, my mother's brother [SOBBING] had a little daughter, four years old. And her name was Eva. My little cousin-- I never saw her after that time, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because all men had to go away to forced labor. I don't know from what age to what age.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you used to play with your little cousin before then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. But it was difficult to get out of the house. So my aunty couldn't-- and we couldn't meet each other because they wasn't live very near. And my grandmother I couldn't see either, because we, we wasn't very near to each other, different parts of the town. And when we had to go to ghetto, my grandmother, mother's side, and, and us, my mother and myself, it was in one cramped condition. But before this was happened, a last time when my father was allowed to come out of the forced labor, which I have the photo-- that was before the-- actually the yellow star they had to wear. My grandmother and grandfather came from the country where they used to live, my father's side. And it was the last time I saw them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you occupy yourself as a-- as a little-- as a child, if you were on your own and your father was working?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was-- I was taught how to do knitting. And I wasn't doing anything useful, but I was always doing something, either knitting or drawing or-- I was always doing something. I was drawing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had this-- I had one little girl in the same courtyard who also-- who also been-- she wasn't Jewish and she wasn't allowed to play with me. But she wanted to. So once-- that was before the yellow star, before we stopped from walking on the street-- she said I take you to church, but don't tell your mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to the church. And I mean I wasn't-- we wasn't religious. We wasn't talking about anything to do with the Christian religion. So I am doing nothing. I didn't know very much about the Jewish religion either. So we went to this church, which was quite near. And was we have to kneel down. And when was all you know [INAUDIBLE] she was showing this and that what's in the-- in the church. And she is just showing me the Christ on the cross and says, you see, that what the Jews done to him. [SOBBING] And when I went home, I didn't know when was that happened. And I was asking my mother, why-- when the Jews done such a thing? And my mother was really shocked. And she says, who said that? So I wouldn't tell her how I know.

speaker: interviewee
text: But of course, I used to go in to the school where everybody was Christian so-- I was Jewish. So she thought I might heard in the school. So she said, well, it's debatable who done it. But don't take much notice of it. And it wasn't happen now. It was happened an awful long time ago. So-- because I felt it may be some of the people I know. You know, it was thought to me it was just happened recently. So that was just one of those experience. And then last time I was in, in school and the children was throwing stone at me, I thought it might have been that something to do with it. It was frightening, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were alone, being an only child, and alone at school, being the only Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --child there. Did you have friends at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, this young age, kids very disciplined. In Hungary those days, school was very disciplined, had to sit straight and two hands behind the back, no talking. Very strict, and if somebody then thought of the lying or haven't done the lesson properly, had to go out and was smacking their hand with the stick. So it was very strict.

speaker: interviewer
text: It wasn't very easy to be friend because of the school, you know? Teachers was very strict. And boys was getting-- had to bend on their desk, and they had smacked on their bottom. Is there anything else you remember about school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- you said that they-- that your family weren't religious.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did Judaism fit in your family life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I just have been told that I'm not Christian. I'm Jewish. And-- or generally, Jews came from then Palestine, which one was Israel then. Now that's it. I didn't even know where it was. And that's all I knew. You know, I didn't know all the story behind. I only learn enough to know. And so anyway, we went to the ghetto. And there wasn't-- I don't know. We had no money, income, no, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] nowhere to go shopping. I really don't where from we had money, or where from we find food to eat. I don't really know how we was managed. I was very young.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the we that went to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? But ?]-- Yes, my mother and my grandmother, the three of us together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father was at this time already taken for forced labor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes. And then from the ghetto, we was collected up, middle of the night, actually. I got a photo from-- a documentary picture, which is-- show in Hungary the Jews was collected up middle of the night from ghettos. So by morning when the other people they talk, we wasn't nowhere. So we was-- in this area, had a brick factory and we was taken there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how long you were in the ghetto for?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't know. I don't know how long we was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any more about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I really don't know how long we was there. And in brick factory, again, we wasn't very long. I mean it was out in the open air. And you know, stage by stage, had to-- things have to be left behind. We just couldn't take-- carry it. And we was always walking on foot. So you know, you can't carry very much. And everybody put something in a case as much as they could. I don't know, most likely food. And when we was in this ghetto-- in that brick factory grounds, we were sleeping there and it was on the grounds. It was really terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were lots of other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: And water had to carry from somewhere. I didn't. Most likely my mother or grandmother brought it. And guess just put some bricks together and put some rubbish in and light it and try to make something to eat on it. I mean how can you make anything serious?

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, from there, it was a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] railway line right there to the brick factory. And there is the place where the wagons was stopped. And you had to go on the wagons. And we was taken to Auschwitz from there. Do you remember what you felt like at--

speaker: interviewee
text: Frightened all the time, really frightened. And of course, everybody was frightened.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long had you been frightened? When, when did you start being frightened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was started really this-- I was just frightened all the time from the time I, I think, when, when I last went to school, the proper school. Really, from then on I was very frightened. And I'm afraid this still goes on with me, because I don't tell people I'm Jewish unless I have to. So if I am among Jewish people, that's different. But when I'm not, I mean. I had a job. I had several jobs. I mean, I never said I'm Jewish. I used to say that I'm from Hungary and being from this country, I don't know nothing about religion. So I have no religion. So I've never said I'm Jewish, you know. And of course, since the war is over, I'm not religious really. I mean I'm-- I wasn't. But I don't believe in these prayers. Because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all these people who was really kept very, very strictly on everything where it should have been done, expected from them, and they was praying all the way to the concentration camps. If God wasn't listening, [SOBBING] I fear it was wasted hope.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'm afraid I don't-- I don't-- I am very Jewish at heart. But I don't keep anything. I just-- I want to just melt into the general public. So I went out of my way to marry somebody who is not Jewish, like my Mr. Parker, who was English.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyway, when I went-- I'll go back to the time we was taken to Auschwitz. Course of the journey, I don't know how long we was going, we had to very tightly crammed up. We have to be sitting on that floor of that-- and we was all hungry and thirsty. And was two German soldiers in the middle. So one half of the wagon divided from the other. I don't know why. But it was a gap. And they were sitting there. And they was drinking something from a bottle, whether it was wine or water, I wouldn't know. But they was eating, eating. And they was kept cutting the crust of the bread off. And put in their bag, most likely with the rubbish. And we were sitting there starving. It was really terrible, really terrible. And then the wagon stopped. Now I have seen sometime picture of people go to visit Auschwitz. I mean, that-- they don't see really what I seen. Because when we got off from that wagons, it was an awful large crowd. I mean so large crowd, it had to queue to the-- I had to turn to the left, and others had to turn to the right. Now all these people who I seen, and turn to the right, I never seen them again. And those people who turned to the left like me, and like my mother, as well as myself, we went--

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this immediately that you got off the, the wagon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Left, right, not even assembling first and then left, right, as you got off?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, it was such a huge crowd. Because each wagon was jam-packed with people and everybody-- and that was boys and old men between as well, and children to old ladies. Now I recognized then that I have been turned-- made to turn to the left, with-- were several German soldiers. But the one who was asking the question, I don't know why, somebody asked me before how old I am. And I said, I'm 13. But of course, I was only 12 and a bit. And as we was coming nearer-- I didn't speak German. And somebody said, if someone asks you how old are you say, 13. And told me in German language. Well, I don't remember it now. So when I get-- got there, I told them-- I said that I was 13. So that's why I'm here. Because really, honestly, truthfully, whoever went to Auschwitz to the left side of the camp, there was only children from age 13. And now going to the Survivor Center in [? Herndon, ?] and I've never met anybody who's younger than me who was in Auschwitz. Maybe in Birkenau, which is on the other side, I don't know. But I only know where I was, there was no children under 13. And we all went [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] into that area, which was really very frightening because somehow or the other, was a funny smell, which I'll later find out was because they burned bodies. Was a funny smell, . and a funny-- it was a frightening place, anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe how it was frightening?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- because it was just-- it was just soil, but not dust. You know, it was hard soil. It was not-- it's not, not-- a stone soil. It's not a stone area you walked. We walked only on, on hard soil with no stone anywhere or grass. And right and left, these barracks, which is wooden huts, but quite large. And I didn't see in that time the inside of it, but they was look, really, like prison camps anyway. And not even windows, only a door on the front and then door in the back, sort of like a shed, enlarged. You know, large sheds sort. I don't know if they still exist, but that's the way they was looked like. And either side, there's such a-- such a huge electric fence double, which I'm told that is full of electricity, so I must not go too near to it. And with watch towers--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did--did you know what Auschwitz-- sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was, you know-- that was Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you- did you know it was-- did you know what Auschwitz was when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, didn't know where we were going. I didn't know the name, nothing. And there's watch towers as well, so you could see-- it was not that high. But you could see people was standing there, looking down. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] uniformed soldiers. And we was taken to a place which room was called washroom. And was a huge room with a crisscross shower heads, every [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] pipe is mixed-- crossed with a shower head. So everybody expected to have a shower. I wasn't speaking German, so I didn't know what is it. But somebody said, maybe we'll have a shower. But that time, we had to take absolutely everything off, totally everything, except the shoes. No, the shoes was allowed on. And had to go somewhere in the area where everybody had their hair shaven off. Every hair shaven off, not just their head. And just had to go forward a little bit, and everybody got a rag given. I don't know what clothes was that, just sort of a rag woman's clothes, which, which was some too big or too small to depend. Because I mean I was only a thin child, I had a big one. So we had to swap it over with some women who was much bigger. I had just the small clothes. And this had a disinfectant smell. And afterwards they told me I have to go to Barrack 16, I think. Either 12 or 16, I'm not sure. So anyway, when I went into this barrack, I seen it-- it's like three tier of bunk, and jam-packed with people, and mostly younger people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your-- [INAUDIBLE] take your mother and grandmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I I wasn't seeing them. I- I- I haven't seen my grandmother ever since. But my mother I did. So I know for sure, somehow or other, in spite of that crowd, I, I had just the feeling she was coming on the same way that I was going that turned to the left, and I'm quite sure I heard the next one have to turn to the left as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was quite sure my mother was somewhere. Because it so fast was going on, I couldn't see among the people I had to hurry up, go, and then nonstop they was [INAUDIBLE] schnell, schnell. This means fast, fast. I think is that mean.

speaker: interviewee
text: And had to keep going fast, fast. Had to keep move on, you know. And I was really frightened, because these soldiers had the weapon under their arm. And people told me who was understand German-- so you see, all age understand German in Hungary. Because in school had to. Before I was old enough to learn German language, they went to school, they learned the German language.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they said, they are not going to be afraid to use it, if not doing as told, so just do what is told. So anyway when, when we came out of this [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, got this rag-- and we all had to go to the barrack number where they was telling us. There every barrack was absolutely jam-packed with women.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I haven't seen any young or old ones. But the youngest are seen, it's sort of like me and older, but still young. Now this barrack I went in there was all, most of them was young girls. And they all was crying. And I was just going crazy. And I went out of that barrack and went one barrack to the other to the other. I was shouting, Mommy, Mommy, [SOBBING] Mrs. Hochhauser in Hungarian. And every barrack I went and shout at top of my voice. There's nobody taking notice of me. I went to the next. I was doing that for ages, because 22 barracks was all together. I went to all this. And I was waiting somebody recognize me. Because they were just laying on these-- there was no blanket or anything. They was just laying on that wooden [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] bunk beds. But large because a lot of people could go on-- on each of those bunk beds. And on the very end of this-- it's called [? C ?] lager-- we'll continue.
